one_line_takeaway: Inter-agent trust in LLM multi-agent systems is a first-class security variable: turning it up boosts task success but monotonically increases unintentional PII over-disclosure (measured by OER/AD over 1,488 A2A chains), and two pluggable defenses can flatten that trust-risk slope by 38-88%.
key_finding: OER rises monotonically with trust across every model-framework cell (average uplift ~+0.26 from tau=0.1 to tau=0.9), and AD is positive everywhere, confirming TVP. Task success also rises with trust (e.g., Llama-3-8B ~0.05-0.22 -> 0.71), yielding an explicit efficiency-safety trade-off. Trust sensitivity is heterogeneous: the trust-risk slope orders as Llama-3-8B > Qwen >= DeepSeek > GPT. Both defenses substantially flatten the slope and cut high-trust exposure (20-50% OER and 38-88% AD reductions) without materially hurting task success. Notably, trust-induced compliance is MOST dangerous in benign collaboration (out-of-scope/emergency requests: OER 0.41, AD 0.077) and LESS dangerous under overt impersonation attacks (OER 0.20, AD 0.028).
core_contribution: The paper introduces and empirically validates the Trust-Vulnerability Paradox (TVP) in LLM-based multi-agent systems: raising inter-agent trust to improve coordination simultaneously enlarges the risk of unintentional over-exposure and over-authorization. Its own contributions are (1) formalizing TVP; (2) a reusable, purpose-built scenario-game dataset (3 macro scenes / 19 sub-scenes) that parameterizes a discrete trust coefficient tau in {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}; (3) two unified metrics grounded in a Minimum Necessary Information (MNI) baseline -- Over-Exposure Rate (OER), the fraction of chains whose output exceeds the MNI baseline, and Authorization Drift (AD), the weighted variance of OER across trust levels (i.e., the slope/sensitivity of leakage to tau); and (4) two low-cost, pluggable defenses (Sensitive-Information Repartitioning and Guardian-Agent enablement). Note: much of the trust-governance framing (TrustAgent taxonomy, zero-trust MAS, generative-agent trust behavior) is cited Related Work, NOT this paper's own contribution; the paper's distinctive move is operationalizing trust as a controlled intervention variable and measuring OER/AD empirically.
threat_model: Unlike the classic attacker-defender paradigm (a malicious agent exfiltrating a known secret from a benign one, which the authors explicitly contrast against), the primary threat here is UNINTENTIONAL over-disclosure driven by benign collaboration. A Seeker-Agent (SK-Agent, project-manager role) requests help from a Custodian-Keeper-Agent (CK-Agent) that holds sensitive data (PII: names, salaries, home addresses, performance ratings, authorization tokens, resource states). The task only requires an aggregate statistic g(Sigma), but under stronger trust the CK-Agent's helpfulness/compliance prior causes it to append individual-level sensitive fields, exceeding the MNI boundary. Trust acts as a risk amplifier along agent-to-agent chains (lateral movement, authorization drift, cascading over-disclosure). The paper additionally examines overtly malicious/impersonation scenarios, but finds these are MORE contained than benign ones.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) 1,488 chains suffice for aggregate trends but are inadequate for tail-risk / fine-grained analysis (they plan to scale to 10^4-10^5 runs). (2) Generation stochasticity persists despite fixed parameters (temperature, context, version drift); future work proposes multi-seed Monte Carlo and hierarchical Bayesian modeling. (3) Trust is only discrete (tau in {0.1,0.5,0.9}); continuous/dynamic tau is left for later. (4) Networking, tools, and memory were disabled to control confounders, limiting ecological validity -- real deployments with tool use and long-horizon collaboration are untested. Additional reviewer-noted concerns: OER depends on a rules+classifier sensitivity detector whose accuracy is not benchmarked; the reference-model definitions of GPT/DeepSeek/Qwen versions are unspecified; and the leakage-probability equation (Eq. 2) is presented as a conceptual reference rather than a fitted model.

datasets_used: Purpose-built scenario-game dataset (authors' own, synthetic/de-identified): 3 macro scenes -- enterprise collaboration (S1: 6 sub-scenes), deep-sea archaeology (S2: 7), Mars colonization (S3: 6) -- totaling 19 controlled sub-scenes
1,488 independent agent-to-agent interaction chains generated over the (S, tau, m, f) grid
quantitative_results: DeepSeek OER rises with tau (0.1->0.9): AgentScope 0.120->0.500, AutoGen 0.379->0.557, LangGraph 0.261->0.468; average uplift ~+0.26

Per-model OER at tau {0.1,0.5,0.9} under AgentScope: GPT 0.05/0.34/0.41 (deltaOER 0.36), DeepSeek 0.12/0.42/0.50 (deltaOER 0.38), Qwen 0.13/0.37/0.56 (deltaOER 0.43), Llama-3-8B 0.05/.../0.71 (deltaOER 0.66)
Authorization Drift (AD) by model (AgentScope): Llama-3-8B 0.0783 (highest), Qwen 0.0310, DeepSeek 0.0268, GPT 0.0243 (lowest); mean ~0.0401
AD by framework (DeepSeek): AutoGen 0.0068 << AgentScope 0.0268 < LangGraph 0.0296
Task success rises with trust, e.g., Llama-3-8B ~0.05/0.22 (no/low trust) -> 0.71 (high trust)

Defense - Sensitive-Information Repartitioning: DeepSeek OER {0.21,0.30,0.39}, AD 0.0055 (79.5% AD reduction; tau=0.9 OER -22%, 0.50->0.39); Llama-3-8B OER {0.15,0.25,0.38}, AD 0.0091 (88.4% AD reduction; tau=0.9 OER -46.5%, 0.71->0.38)

Defense - Guardian-Agent enablement: DeepSeek OER {0.10,0.33,0.40}, AD 0.0165 (38.4% AD reduction; tau=0.9 OER -20%); Llama-3-8B OER {0.09,0.24,0.36}, AD 0.0128 (83.6% AD reduction; tau=0.9 OER -49.3%)
Overall defenses achieve 20-50% OER and 38-88% AD reductions
Scenario effect: out-of-scope/emergency requests OER 0.41 / AD 0.077 (highest risk); overt impersonation attacks OER 0.20 / AD 0.028 (more contained)
Length-controlled resampling of residual tau=0.1 OER: r < 0.12, p > 0.1, ruling out a length-risk artifact
baselines_compared: Minimum Necessary Information (MNI) principle as the safety baseline (A* = g(Sigma), often set to empty set for a conservative upper bound) against which OER is measured
No-defense condition vs. Sensitive-Information Repartitioning vs. Guardian-Agent enablement (ablation of the two defenses)
Cross-model comparison: DeepSeek, Qwen, GPT, Llama-3-8B (local)
Conceptual comparison table (Table I) vs. prior trust-in-MAS studies (Chintala 2024, Yu et al. 2025, Xie et al. 2024, Cao et al. 2025, He et al. 2025) -- positioning only, not a quantitative baseline
